labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

labels.name: wontfix
labels.description: This will not be worked on

issues.title: Hash algorithm choice: SHA-384 or SHA-512
issues.author: panva
issues.body:
The hash algorithm chosen by this document is currently SHA-512. The working group should determine whether to define SHA-384 or SHA-512.

This TODO appears in the Introduction section of the spec.

issues.title: Dual-hash coexistence: allow or prohibit both old and new hash values simultaneously
issues.author: panva
issues.body:
Several places in the spec raise the question of whether both the existing (SHA-256-based) and new (SHA-512-based) hash values should be allowed to coexist simultaneously. This may be useful during transition periods but needs a clear decision.

Affected areas:

1. **mTLS certificate confirmation** - RFC 7800 Section 3.1 does not preclude the presence of both x5t#S256 and x5t#S512 in the same cnf claim. Including both would represent two different hash confirmations of the same certificate.

2. **DPoP key confirmation** - Similarly, both jkt and jkt#S512 could appear in the same cnf claim as two different hash confirmations of the same key.

3. **DPoP access token hash** - Both ath and ath#S512 could appear in the same DPoP proof JWT as two different hash confirmations of the same access token.

The working group should determine whether to prohibit or allow this coexistence in each case.

comments.author: MichaelFraser99
comments.body: I'm in favour of allowing co-existence for migration purposes

issues.title: Notational Conventions
issues.author: MichaelFraser99
issues.body:
In [Section 2](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7636#section-2) of PKCE, there is text defining the following:

```
STRING denotes a sequence of zero or more ASCII [[RFC20](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc20)] characters.

OCTETS denotes a sequence of zero or more octets.

ASCII(STRING) denotes the octets of the ASCII [[RFC20](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc20)] representation
of STRING where STRING is a sequence of zero or more ASCII
characters.

BASE64URL-ENCODE(OCTETS) denotes the base64url encoding of OCTETS,
per [Appendix A](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7636#appendix-A), producing a STRING.

BASE64URL-DECODE(STRING) denotes the base64url decoding of STRING,
per [Appendix A](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7636#appendix-A), producing a sequence of octets.

SHA256(OCTETS) denotes a SHA2 256-bit hash [RFC6234](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6234) of OCTETS.
```

This document should consider including an analogue. Suggested text:

```
The key words "BASE64URL-ENCODE", "ASCII", and "OCTETS" in this document
are to be interpreted as described in [RFC7636](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7636#section-2).

SHA-512(OCTETS) denotes a SHA2 512-bit hash [RFC6234](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6234) of OCTETS.
```

For this to work, the definition of S512 should be updated to:

```text
S512:  code_challenge = BASE64URL-ENCODE(SHA-512(ASCII(code_verifier)))
```

issues.title: Alignment between ath_methods and dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported
issues.author: MichaelFraser99
issues.body:
This document defines both `dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported` and `ath_methods` as mechanisms of listing which access token has methods a given deployment supports. There is not however any text mandating they contain the same values. Is this intentional? 

Is there a valid scenario where a deployment may list `ath` and `ath#S512` in the `ath_methods` list while just specifying `ath#S512` within `dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported`?

issues.title: Behaviour of the ath_methods property when a server supports ath#S512
issues.author: MichaelFraser99
issues.body:
The text `When ath_methods is absent, the Client MUST use ath.` opens us up to the following scenario:

Take a deployment that chooses to list `ath#S512` as an option in `dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported` and then subsequently respond with a `WWW-Authenticate: DPoP challenge` in order to provide a nonce for the client to use and does NOT include the `ath_methods` parameter (its only marked as a MAY)

```
A Resource Server MAY signal the acceptable access token hash methods
   by including the ath_methods parameter in the WWW-Authenticate: DPoP
   challenge.
```

In such a scenario, the client would then be expected to use `ath` instead per the above text. 

I suggest that we include some text requiring the presence of the `ath_methods` parameter in `WWW-Authenticate` challenges when an implementation supports `ath#S512`. Suggested text:

```
A Resource Server MAY signal the acceptable access token hash methods
   by including the ath_methods parameter in the WWW-Authenticate: DPoP
   challenge. When an implementation chooses to support `ath#S256`, 
   the `ath_methods` parameter MUST be used in the `WWW-Authenticate: DPoP` 
   challenge.
```

pulls.title: add S512 PKCE related notational conventions
pulls.author: panva
pulls.body:
Import BASE64URL-ENCODE, ASCII, and OCTETS notation from RFC 7636 Section 2, define SHA-512(OCTETS) citing RFC 6234, and update the S512 code challenge formula to use BASE64URL-ENCODE consistently.

Fixes #3

cc @MichaelFraser99

pulls.title: require ath_methods in challenges when RS does not support ath
pulls.author: panva
pulls.body:
Clarify ath_methods behavior when a Resource Server supports only ath#S512. A RS that does not support the default ath claim must now include ath_methods in WWW-Authenticate challenges. Clients may also use previously obtained RS metadata to determine the correct method when ath_methods is absent from the challenge.

Closes #5

cc @MichaelFraser99

comments.body:
The content is clear to me here. I do wonder if we can reduce the wordiness of it a bit:

Instead of

```
... When `ath_methods`
is absent and the Client has not obtained the Resource Server's
`dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported` metadata, the Client
MUST use `ath`. When `ath_methods` is absent but the Client has
previously obtained the Resource Server's metadata, the Client
MUST use a method from
`dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported`. ...
```

How about

```
... When `ath_methods` is absent: 
If the Client is aware of the Resource Server's 
`dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported` metadata, the Client 
MUST use a method from that set. Otherwise, `ath` MUST be used.
```

Just to aid readability a little

pulls.title: add consistency requirement between ath_methods and dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported
pulls.author: panva
pulls.body:
Clarify that dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported represents static general capabilities while ath_methods serves as the runtime authoritative signal. Require ath_methods values to be a subset of dpop_access_token_hash_methods_supported.

Closes #4

cc @MichaelFraser99
